problem:
Patient Records: Input:
age: 49. gender: M.  
Name:  ___               Unit No:   ___
 
Admission Date:  ___              Discharge Date:   ___
 
Date of Birth:  ___             Sex:   M
 
Service: OTOLARYNGOLOGY
 
Allergies: 
lisinopril
 
Attending: ___.
 
Chief Complaint:
Throat swelling

 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Fiberoptic laryngoscopy

 
History of Present Illness:
___ with history of asthma, diabetes, hypertension presents with 
sensation of throat swelling. Patient states that for the past 
___ weeks he has had rhinorrhea especially in the morning, as 
well as a cough productive of whitish sputum. He states that his 
cough is worse in the early morning hours, around 0100-0200. 
Denies any fevers throughout this time. His cough worsened last 
night, developed sore throat and swelling in his throat this 
morning along with a sensation of foreign body. He states that 
he has had several episodes with similar symptoms over the past 
___ years, but usually they either self resolve, or resolve with 
benadryl. Most recently he had an episode 7 days prior to 
presentation consisting of upper lip swelling last a few hours 
that was relieved with benadryl. Denies any recent medication 
changes, travel, new soaps or detergents, or alterations in 
diet. This morning he took benadryl with no relief in his 
symptoms, and this episode was more severe than previous. 
Endorses a small amount of SOB due to tightness. Denies CP, 
fevers, vomiting. He does take lisinopril, has been taking it 
for approximately one year. 

Presented to OSH received decadron, benadryl, pepcid. Patient 
states that at presentation to OSH, his swelling was so severe 
that he was unable to talk, and had to communicate with staff 
there through writing. Normal soft tissue x-ray per report.

In the ED, initial VS were: 98.6 95 126/77 16 99%RA. Patient 
recieved inhaled racemic epinephrine x1 and ENT was consulted 
who scoped him and deemed that his swelling appears 
hypopharyngeal with mild epiglottal swelling.  Vocal cords 
appeared normal. Patient continued to be stridorous in the ED, 
so MICU bed was requested.  No plans for intubation at that 
time. 
 
On arrival to the MICU, patient states that he feels markedly 
improved ("If I was a ___ at OSH, I'm now a ___. He endorses 
a mild discomfort in his throat, but this is far less than he 
had been experiencing earlier today.

 
Past Medical History:
HTN, DM, asthma

 
Social History:
___
Family History:
Paternal history of DM, asthma, HTN

 
Physical Exam:
ADMISSION PHYSICAL EXAM:
Vitals: T: 98.3 BP: 141/93 P: 109 R: 18 O2: 98%RA
General: Alert, oriented, no acute distress  
HEENT: Sclera anicteric, MMM, oropharynx is narrowed, Mallampati 
4, edematous uvula, EOMI, PERRL  
Neck: supple, JVP not elevated, no LAD, no stridor appreciated
CV: Tachycardic but regular rhythm, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, 
rubs, gallops  
Lungs: Diffuse inspiratory and expiratory wheezes in all lung 
fields
Abdomen: soft, non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds present, 
no organomegaly  
GU: no foley  
Ext: warm, well perfused, 2+ pulses, no clubbing, cyanosis or 
edema
Skin: No evidence of urticaria or rash  
Neuro: CNII-XII intact, ___ strength upper/lower extremities, 
grossly normal sensation, 2+ reflexes bilaterally, gait 
deferred, finger-to-nose intact 

DISCHARGE PHYSICAL EXAM: 
General: Alert, oriented, no acute distress  
HEENT: Sclera anicteric, MMM, oropharynx is narrowed, Mallampati 
4, edematous uvula, EOMI, PERRL  
Neck: supple, JVP not elevated, no LAD, no stridor appreciated
CV: Tachycardic but regular rhythm, normal S1 + S2, no murmurs, 
rubs, gallops  
Lungs: CTAB
Abdomen: soft, non-tender, non-distended, bowel sounds present, 
no organomegaly  
GU: no foley  
Ext: warm, well perfused, 2+ pulses, no clubbing, cyanosis or 
edema
Skin: No evidence of urticaria or rash  
Neuro: CNII-XII intact, ___ strength upper/lower extremities, 
grossly normal sensation, 2+ reflexes bilaterally, gait 
deferred, finger-to-nose intact

 
Pertinent Results:
ADMISSION LABS: 
___ 11:08PM   WBC-9.8 RBC-4.78 HGB-15.0 HCT-42.0 MCV-88 
MCH-31.3 MCHC-35.6* RDW-12.6
___ 11:08PM   NEUTS-92.4* LYMPHS-5.3* MONOS-0.6* EOS-1.6 
BASOS-0.1
___ 11:08PM   PLT COUNT-203
___ 11:08PM   ___ PTT-35.6 ___
___ 08:30PM   GLUCOSE-108* UREA N-9 CREAT-1.1 SODIUM-137 
POTASSIUM-4.3 CHLORIDE-97 TOTAL CO2-27 ANION GAP-17
___ 08:30PM   cTropnT-<0.01
___ 08:30PM   WBC-7.8 RBC-5.00 HGB-15.3 HCT-44.2 MCV-88 
MCH-30.5 MCHC-34.6 RDW-12.6
___ 08:30PM   PLT COUNT-218
___ 08:30PM   ___ PTT-36.4 ___

IMAGING:
- CXR ___: IMPRESSION: No evidence of acute cardiac 
abnormalities

 
Brief Hospital Course:
___ with h/o HTN, diabetes, and asthma presents with cough, sore 
throat and sensation throat swelling x1 day. 

# Airway swelling: Patient given decadron, diphenhydramine and 
famotidine at OSH for concern of angioedema and upper airway 
swelling, transferred here for ENT and anesthesia evaluation 
should intubation be required. Receieved nebulized racemic 
epinephrine in the ED here. Upon arrival to the MICU the patient 
was subjectively improved. The etiology of his angioedema was 
unclear, but there was concern for contribution of symptoms from 
ACEi. As a result, his ACEi was discontinued while in the MICU, 
and was not restarted upon discharge. He was treated with 
steroids, benadryl, and famotidine. ENT evaulation on the 
morning of discharge revealed resolution of edema and he was 
discharged home from the MICU. 

# Cough/Rhinorrhea: Patient's complaints of cough and rhinorrhea 
recently were most likely due to URI. CXR was without evidence 
of PNA. He was treated with saline nasal spray while in the 
MICU. 

# Tachycardia: Likely in the acute setting of receiving IV 
steroids and epinephrine. Appears to be sinus tachycardia on 
monitor. Troponin from the ED was negative.

# Asthma: Upon arrival to the MICU he was noted to have diffuse 
wheezes on exam. He was given Albuterol nebulizers as needed.

# Diabetes: No active issues. He was discharged on his home dose 
of metformin. 

# HTN: Lisinopril was discontinued upon arrival to the MICU. His 
blood pressure remained within the normal range. He was not 
restarted on any additional anti-hypertensive medications prior 
to discharge. 

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
- Patient will likely need treatment with alternative 
antihypertensive medication, given discontinuation of 
lisinopril. MICU team elected to defer to PCP on this decision.

 
Medications on Admission:
Metformin 500mg BID
Lisinopril unknown dose
Albuterol MDI prn

 
Discharge Medications:
1. DiphenhydrAMINE 25 mg PO Q6H:PRN sob, hives, swelling 
2. Famotidine 20 mg PO BID 
RX *famotidine 20 mg 1 tablet(s) by mouth twice a day Disp #*30 
Tablet Refills:*0
3. PredniSONE 40 mg PO DAILY Duration: 4 Days 
RX *prednisone 20 mg 2 tablet(s) by mouth daily Disp #*8 Tablet 
Refills:*0
4. Albuterol Inhaler ___ PUFF IH Q4H:PRN sob, wheeze 
Seek medical attention if you are using this more than 3 times a 
day. 
RX *albuterol sulfate 90 mcg ___ puffs inhaled every 4 hours 
Disp #*1 Inhaler Refills:*0
5. Fluticasone Propionate 110mcg 2 PUFF IH BID 
Use this daily to help control your asthma. 
RX *fluticasone [Flovent HFA] 110 mcg/actuation 2 puffs inhaled 
twice a day Disp #*1 Inhaler Refills:*0
6. MetFORMIN (Glucophage) 500 mg PO BID 

 
Discharge Disposition:
Home
 
Discharge Diagnosis:
Primary: Angioedema, asthma

 
Discharge Condition:
Mental Status: Clear and coherent.
Level of Consciousness: Alert and interactive.
Activity Status: Ambulatory - Independent.

 
Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. ___,

 It was a pleasure taking care of you during your 
hospitalization.  You were admitted because of swelling in your 
throat (angioedema) that was causing difficulty breathing.  You 
were admitted to the Medical Intensive Care Unit so we could 
closely monitor your breathing.  You did very well overnight and 
were treated with steroids, benadryl, and famotidine 
(anti-histamines) which helped to reduce the swelling.  We think 
this swelling was a side effect of your lisinopril.  You should 
not take lisinopril or other medicines in this class (ACE 
inhibitors) ever again as the swelling could happen again 
causing difficulty breathing.  You will need to talk to your PCP 
about starting ___ different blood pressure medicine.  You should 
also talk to your PCP about seeing an Allergy specialist to 
determine if there is another reason for the swelling you had.
  You were also wheezing when you were admitted.  This was 
because of your asthma.  You improved after a treatment with 
albuterol.  Your asthma can be better controlled if you take 
your Flovent inhaler every day - this helps control asthma 
symptoms.  We will give you a prescription for an albuterol 
inhaler that you should only use when you are having symptoms 
from your asthma.
 
Followup Instructions:
___
 

Output:

Summarize the key diagnostic information and significant results based on the patients’ multiple health (long) records during hospitalization.
thinking: The student's answer involves a case study with multiple stages: diagnostic information, hospital management, and discharge plan. These parts of the answer form a sequential flow of reasoning and do not include backtracking or alternative solution paths, so all parts should be retained. I need to add '\n' to split each step according to the specific instructions.
response:
The patient is a 49-year-old male with a history of HTN, DM, and asthma who presented with acute angioedema and upper airway swelling, likely secondary to Lisinopril use. 
 Significant findings included hypopharyngeal and epiglottal swelling noted on fiberoptic laryngoscopy and diffuse wheezing on lung exam. 
 Laboratory results were notable for neutrophilia (92.4%), while cardiac workup (Troponin, CXR) was negative. 
 Management involved airway monitoring in the MICU, treatment with corticosteroids (Decadron, Prednisone), antihistamines (Benadryl, Famotidine), and racemic epinephrine. 
 Lisinopril was discontinued permanently. 
 The patient's asthma was managed with Albuterol and the addition of Flovent. 
 He was discharged home in stable condition with instructions to strictly avoid ACE inhibitors and follow up with his PCP for alternative hypertension management and an allergy consultation. 
 Answer: Discharge Summary: 
 Patient: 49-year-old male 
 Admitting Diagnosis: Angioedema, Asthma exacerbation 
 Key Diagnostic Information: The patient presented with a severe sensation of throat swelling and inability to speak, requiring transfer from an outside hospital (OSH). 
 Fiberoptic laryngoscopy performed by ENT confirmed hypopharyngeal edema and mild epiglottal swelling, though vocal cords remained normal. 
 Admission labs revealed a white blood cell count of 9.8 K/uL with 92.4% neutrophils. 
 Chest X-ray showed no acute cardiac or pulmonary abnormalities, and serial troponins were negative. 
 Physical exam on admission was significant for an edematous uvula and diffuse inspiratory/expiratory wheezing. 
 Hospital Course and Management: The patient was admitted to the Medical Intensive Care Unit (MICU) for close airway monitoring. 
 He received intravenous Decadron, Benadryl, and Famotidine, as well as nebulized racemic epinephrine in the Emergency Department. 
 Given his year-long history of Lisinopril use, the angioedema was attributed to ACE-inhibitor (ACEi) therapy. 
 Lisinopril was immediately discontinued. 
 His asthma was managed with Albuterol nebulizers and he was started on a daily Flovent inhaler for better long-term control. 
 By the morning of discharge, his airway edema had resolved, and his lungs were clear to auscultation. 
 Discharge Plan: The patient was discharged home on a 4-day course of Prednisone (40mg daily), scheduled Famotidine, and as-needed Benadryl. 
 He was instructed to never take Lisinopril or any other ACE inhibitor again due to the risk of life-threatening recurrence. 
 He will continue Metformin for diabetes and was provided with Albuterol and Flovent for asthma management. 
 He is to follow up with his primary care physician to initiate a new antihypertensive regimen and consider an allergy referral.

The patient is a 49-year-old male with a history of HTN, DM, and asthma who presented with acute angioedema and upper airway swelling, likely secondary to Lisinopril use. Significant findings included hypopharyngeal and epiglottal swelling noted on fiberoptic laryngoscopy and diffuse wheezing on lung exam. Laboratory results were notable for neutrophilia (92.4%), while cardiac workup (Troponin, CXR) was negative. Management involved airway monitoring in the MICU, treatment with corticosteroids (Decadron, Prednisone), antihistamines (Benadryl, Famotidine), and racemic epinephrine. Lisinopril was discontinued permanently. The patient's asthma was managed with Albuterol and the addition of Flovent. He was discharged home in stable condition with instructions to strictly avoid ACE inhibitors and follow up with his PCP for alternative hypertension management and an allergy consultation.

Answer:Discharge Summary:

Patient: 49-year-old male
Admitting Diagnosis: Angioedema, Asthma exacerbation

Key Diagnostic Information: The patient presented with a severe sensation of throat swelling and inability to speak, requiring transfer from an outside hospital (OSH). Fiberoptic laryngoscopy performed by ENT confirmed hypopharyngeal edema and mild epiglottal swelling, though vocal cords remained normal. Admission labs revealed a white blood cell count of 9.8 K/uL with 92.4% neutrophils. Chest X-ray showed no acute cardiac or pulmonary abnormalities, and serial troponins were negative. Physical exam on admission was significant for an edematous uvula and diffuse inspiratory/expiratory wheezing.

Hospital Course and Management: The patient was admitted to the Medical Intensive Care Unit (MICU) for close airway monitoring. He received intravenous Decadron, Benadryl, and Famotidine, as well as nebulized racemic epinephrine in the Emergency Department. Given his year-long history of Lisinopril use, the angioedema was attributed to ACE-inhibitor (ACEi) therapy. Lisinopril was immediately discontinued. His asthma was managed with Albuterol nebulizers and he was started on a daily Flovent inhaler for better long-term control. By the morning of discharge, his airway edema had resolved, and his lungs were clear to auscultation. 

Discharge Plan: The patient was discharged home on a 4-day course of Prednisone (40mg daily), scheduled Famotidine, and as-needed Benadryl. He was instructed to never take Lisinopril or any other ACE inhibitor again due to the risk of life-threatening recurrence. He will continue Metformin for diabetes and was provided with Albuterol and Flovent for asthma management. He is to follow up with his primary care physician to initiate a new antihypertensive regimen and consider an allergy referral.

thinking: The student's answer contains a sequential normal flow based on the diagnostic summary. There are no backtracking or alternative paths presented. All the relevant steps in the reasoning, such as clinical presentation, diagnostic findings, treatment, and follow-up, were cohesive and cumulatively led to the final summary. I need to add '\n' to split each reasoning step for better organization and grading clarity.
response:
Answer: This 49-year-old male with a history of hypertension, diabetes, and asthma was admitted due to acute throat swelling, shortness of breath, and stridor, symptoms that were severe enough to prevent verbal communication upon initial presentation.
 A key diagnostic fiberoptic laryngoscopy revealed hypopharyngeal and mild epiglottal edema with normal vocal cords, while physical examination confirmed a narrowed oropharynx (Mallampati 4), an edematous uvula, and diffuse inspiratory/expiratory wheezing.
 Significant laboratory findings included neutrophilia (92.4%) and an elevated anion gap (17), though cardiac workup was negative with a normal chest X-ray and troponin levels.
 The primary diagnosis was determined to be angioedema, strongly suspected to be secondary to the use of the ACE inhibitor lisinopril, which was subsequently discontinued.
 During his stay in the Medical Intensive Care Unit, the patient showed rapid improvement following treatment with IV steroids, H1 and H2 antihistamines, and nebulized racemic epinephrine.
 His secondary asthma exacerbation was managed with albuterol and the initiation of a maintenance inhaler.
 He was discharged in stable condition with instructions to permanently avoid all ACE inhibitors and follow up with his primary care physician for alternative hypertension management.

The response must start with 'Answer:' and avoid specific bold headers as requested.

Answer:Answer: This 49-year-old male with a history of hypertension, diabetes, and asthma was admitted due to acute throat swelling, shortness of breath, and stridor, symptoms that were severe enough to prevent verbal communication upon initial presentation. A key diagnostic fiberoptic laryngoscopy revealed hypopharyngeal and mild epiglottal edema with normal vocal cords, while physical examination confirmed a narrowed oropharynx (Mallampati 4), an edematous uvula, and diffuse inspiratory/expiratory wheezing. Significant laboratory findings included neutrophilia (92.4%) and an elevated anion gap (17), though cardiac workup was negative with a normal chest X-ray and troponin levels. The primary diagnosis was determined to be angioedema, strongly suspected to be secondary to the use of the ACE inhibitor lisinopril, which was subsequently discontinued. During his stay in the Medical Intensive Care Unit, the patient showed rapid improvement following treatment with IV steroids, H1 and H2 antihistamines, and nebulized racemic epinephrine. His secondary asthma exacerbation was managed with albuterol and the initiation of a maintenance inhaler. He was discharged in stable condition with instructions to permanently avoid all ACE inhibitors and follow up with his primary care physician for alternative hypertension management.

Output:

Summarize the key diagnostic information and significant results based on the patients’ multiple health (long) records during hospitalization.
thinking: The student's response provides all required information in a coherent and progressive narrative format. There is no evidence of backtracking or multiple possibilities that would require removing any sections. The details synthesize the patient's case succinctly following the discharge summary format. I need to add line breaks to split each reasoning step while maintaining the logical flow.
response:
The patient is a 49-year-old male with a medical history of hypertension, asthma, and diabetes mellitus who presented with acute throat swelling, a sore throat, and a sensation of a foreign body in his throat. 
 Physical examination on admission was significant for a narrowed oropharynx with an edematous uvula (Mallampati 4) and diffuse inspiratory and expiratory wheezes. 
 Fiberoptic laryngoscopy demonstrated hypopharyngeal and mild epiglottal swelling with normal vocal cords. 
 During his stay in the Medical Intensive Care Unit (MICU), he was treated with intravenous steroids, antihistamines (diphenhydramine and famotidine), and nebulized racemic epinephrine, resulting in rapid symptomatic improvement. 
 The etiology of the angioedema was identified as a likely adverse reaction to lisinopril, which was subsequently discontinued. 
 His asthma was managed with albuterol nebulizers as needed. 
 The patient was discharged in stable condition with primary diagnoses of angioedema and asthma. 
 His discharge regimen included a four-day course of prednisone, daily famotidine, PRN diphenhydramine, and metformin. 
 For asthma maintenance, he was initiated on daily fluticasone (Flovent) and provided with a rescue albuterol inhaler. 
 He was strictly instructed to avoid all ACE inhibitors and to follow up with his primary care physician for alternative blood pressure management and an allergist for further diagnostic evaluation.
candidate:
The user wants a concise, professional medical discharge summary for a 49-year-old male patient hospitalized for angioedema. I need to synthesize the demographics, physical findings (Mallampati 4, edematous uvula, wheezing), diagnostic results (laryngoscopy findings), hospital course (MICU, steroids, antihistamines), the suspected cause (ACE inhibitor/Lisinopril), and the discharge plan (medication changes and follow-up). The response must start with 'Answer:' and follow specific formatting rules.

Answer:Answer: The patient is a 49-year-old male with a medical history of hypertension, asthma, and diabetes mellitus who presented with acute throat swelling, a sore throat, and a sensation of a foreign body in his throat. Physical examination on admission was significant for a narrowed oropharynx with an edematous uvula (Mallampati 4) and diffuse inspiratory and expiratory wheezes. Fiberoptic laryngoscopy demonstrated hypopharyngeal and mild epiglottal swelling with normal vocal cords. During his stay in the Medical Intensive Care Unit (MICU), he was treated with intravenous steroids, antihistamines (diphenhydramine and famotidine), and nebulized racemic epinephrine, resulting in rapid symptomatic improvement. The etiology of the angioedema was identified as a likely adverse reaction to lisinopril, which was subsequently discontinued. His asthma was managed with albuterol nebulizers as needed. The patient was discharged in stable condition with primary diagnoses of angioedema and asthma. His discharge regimen included a four-day course of prednisone, daily famotidine, PRN diphenhydramine, and metformin. For asthma maintenance, he was initiated on daily fluticasone (Flovent) and provided with a rescue albuterol inhaler. He was strictly instructed to avoid all ACE inhibitors and to follow up with his primary care physician for alternative blood pressure management and an allergist for further diagnostic evaluation.

thinking: The student's answer outlines a medical case without alternative paths or backtracking. It provides a comprehensive sequence of events and treatments that led to the patient's condition and resolution. Steps are sequential and necessary to understand the case. I also need to add a line break to split each step.
response:
The patient is a 49-year-old male who presented with acute throat swelling, shortness of breath, and a foreign body sensation. 
Fiberoptic laryngoscopy revealed hypopharyngeal and mild epiglottal swelling with normal-appearing vocal cords, while physical examination noted an edematous uvula (Mallampati 4) and diffuse respiratory wheezing. 
Laboratory evaluation was significant for neutrophilia (92.4%), with a chest X-ray showing no acute abnormalities. 
The clinical presentation of angioedema was determined to be likely due to his use of the ACE inhibitor lisinopril. 
Following treatment with corticosteroids, antihistamines, racemic epinephrine, and albuterol, the patient's airway edema resolved and his asthma symptoms improved. 
He was discharged in stable condition with instructions to strictly avoid ACE inhibitors and to follow up with his primary care provider for alternative antihypertensive management and asthma control.

Answer:The patient is a 49-year-old male who presented with acute throat swelling, shortness of breath, and a foreign body sensation. Fiberoptic laryngoscopy revealed hypopharyngeal and mild epiglottal swelling with normal-appearing vocal cords, while physical examination noted an edematous uvula (Mallampati 4) and diffuse respiratory wheezing. Laboratory evaluation was significant for neutrophilia (92.4%), with a chest X-ray showing no acute abnormalities. The clinical presentation of angioedema was determined to be likely due to his use of the ACE inhibitor lisinopril. Following treatment with corticosteroids, antihistamines, racemic epinephrine, and albuterol, the patient's airway edema resolved and his asthma symptoms improved. He was discharged in stable condition with instructions to strictly avoid ACE inhibitors and to follow up with his primary care provider for alternative antihypertensive management and asthma control.

Brief Hospital Course:
___ with h/o HTN, diabetes, and asthma presents with cough, sore 
throat and sensation throat swelling x1 day. 

# Airway swelling: Patient given decadron, diphenhydramine and 
famotidine at OSH for concern of angioedema and upper airway 
swelling, transferred here for ENT and anesthesia evaluation 
should intubation be required. Receieved nebulized racemic 
epinephrine in the ED here. Upon arrival to the MICU the patient 
was subjectively improved. The etiology of his angioedema was 
unclear, but there was concern for contribution of symptoms from 
ACEi. As a result, his ACEi was discontinued while in the MICU, 
and was not restarted upon discharge. He was treated with 
steroids, benadryl, and famotidine. ENT evaulation on the 
morning of discharge revealed resolution of edema and he was 
discharged home from the MICU. 

# Cough/Rhinorrhea: Patient's complaints of cough and rhinorrhea 
recently were most likely due to URI. CXR was without evidence 
of PNA. He was treated with saline nasal spray while in the 
MICU. 

# Tachycardia: Likely in the acute setting of receiving IV 
steroids and epinephrine. Appears to be sinus tachycardia on 
monitor. Troponin from the ED was negative.

# Asthma: Upon arrival to the MICU he was noted to have diffuse 
wheezes on exam. He was given Albuterol nebulizers as needed.

# Diabetes: No active issues. He was discharged on his home dose 
of metformin. 

# HTN: Lisinopril was discontinued upon arrival to the MICU. His 
blood pressure remained within the normal range. He was not 
restarted on any additional anti-hypertensive medications prior 
to discharge. 

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
- Patient will likely need treatment with alternative 
antihypertensive medication, given discontinuation of

Discharge Instructions:
Dear Mr. ___,

 It was a pleasure taking care of you during your 
hospitalization.  You were admitted because of swelling in your 
throat (angioedema) that was causing difficulty breathing.  You 
were admitted to the Medical Intensive Care Unit so we could 
closely monitor your breathing.  You did very well overnight and 
were treated with steroids, benadryl, and famotidine 
(anti-histamines) which helped to reduce the swelling.  We think 
this swelling was a side effect of your lisinopril.  You should 
not take lisinopril or other medicines in this class (ACE 
inhibitors) ever again as the swelling could happen again 
causing difficulty breathing.  You will need to talk to your PCP 
about starting ___ different blood pressure medicine.  You should 
also talk to your PCP about seeing an Allergy specialist to 
determine if there is another reason for the swelling you had.
  You were also wheezing when you were admitted.  This was 
because of your asthma.  You improved after a treatment with

Output:

Summarize the key diagnostic information and significant results based on the patients’ multiple health (long) records during hospitalization.
thinking: The task involves ensuring the post-processed answer is concise and clear without altering the correctness of the diagnosis and management steps listed for the medical discharge summary. The student's answer follows a normal flow, presenting the patient's case in a structured manner without backtracking or alternative solution paths. Each part of the summary is essential for understanding the patient's condition and treatment, so all steps must be retained in the post-processed result. I also need to add '\n' to split each step.
response:
The patient is a 49-year-old male who presented with severe throat swelling, a sensation of a foreign body, and difficulty speaking, occurring despite home treatment with diphenhydramine. 
 Medical history includes hypertension, diabetes, and asthma, with lisinopril use for the past year. 
 Fiberoptic laryngoscopy revealed significant hypopharyngeal and mild epiglottal edema with normal vocal cords, while physical examination noted a narrowed oropharynx, an edematous uvula, and diffuse wheezing. 
 This presentation was diagnostic of angioedema, likely secondary to lisinopril, complicated by an asthma exacerbation. 
 The patient was managed in the Medical Intensive Care Unit with corticosteroids, antihistamines, racemic epinephrine, and albuterol. 
 Following the resolution of airway edema and normalization of breath sounds, he was discharged in stable condition with a permanent contraindication to ACE inhibitors and instructions to seek alternative antihypertensive therapy via his primary care provider.
candidate:
The objective was to create a concise medical discharge summary for a 49-year-old male patient admitted for throat swelling. The summary had to include specific laryngoscopy findings (hypopharyngeal and epiglottal edema), identify lisinopril-induced angioedema as the likely diagnosis, and follow a strict opening sentence format. The resulting text covers the clinical presentation, diagnostic findings, management in the ICU, and the final discharge plan while maintaining a single-paragraph structure.

Answer:The patient is a 49-year-old male who presented with severe throat swelling, a sensation of a foreign body, and difficulty speaking, occurring despite home treatment with diphenhydramine. Medical history includes hypertension, diabetes, and asthma, with lisinopril use for the past year. Fiberoptic laryngoscopy revealed significant hypopharyngeal and mild epiglottal edema with normal vocal cords, while physical examination noted a narrowed oropharynx, an edematous uvula, and diffuse wheezing. This presentation was diagnostic of angioedema, likely secondary to lisinopril, complicated by an asthma exacerbation. The patient was managed in the Medical Intensive Care Unit with corticosteroids, antihistamines, racemic epinephrine, and albuterol. Following the resolution of airway edema and normalization of breath sounds, he was discharged in stable condition with a permanent contraindication to ACE inhibitors and instructions to seek alternative antihypertensive therapy via his primary care provider.
